Step 5 of 5  ·  Hamilton Urban Forest Analysis

Urban Forest Vulnerability & Replanting Strategy

Integrating built environment stress, climate vulnerability, biodiversity pressure, and ash-borer risk into a single ward-level action plan — identifying where Hamilton must plant first, and which species will survive and thrive through 2080.

Replanting Priority ModelEmerald Ash Borer RiskSpecies Recommendations15-Ward Action Plan2030 · 2050 · 2080 Horizons

🗺️

How the Replanting Priority Score Works

The replanting priority score brings every preceding analysis together into one actionable ward-level ranking. Four drivers are combined using weights that reflect their relative importance for long-term urban forest resilience:

Replanting Priority (t) = 0.40 × Built Environment Stress + 0.35 × Climate Vulnerability (t) + 0.15 × Low Diversity Pressure + 0.10 × Biodiversity Pressure

Built Environment Stress (40%) — the dominant factor. Combines road stress (arterial road share) and zoning stress (built land share). Trees in dense urban environments face compounded heat, pollution, and physical root stress.

Climate Vulnerability (35%) — the ISA-adjusted vulnerability scores from Step 4, time-indexed to 2030, 2050, or 2080.

Low Diversity Pressure (15%) — wards with fewer total species have less ecological buffer. The fewer species present, the higher the pressure to diversify.

Biodiversity Pressure (10%) — species and genus dominance violations from the Santamour analysis. High Acer monoculture concentration pushes this score up.

Figure 1 — Replanting priority score by ward for 2030, 2050, and 2080. Ward 3 leads at 0.966 across all three horizons. Wards 9, 11, 12, 13, and 15 consistently rank lowest — they require monitoring but not immediate crisis-level intervention.

📍

Species Dominance Patterns by Ward

Before computing the replanting score, species and genus dominance was mapped across all 15 wards to identify exactly which wards — and which dominant species — are driving monoculture risk. Norway Maple is the most common dominant species but the pattern varies by ward.

Figure 2 — Maximum species share per ward. The dashed line marks the 10% Santamour cap. Most wards exceed it, with Ward 7 (17%) and Ward 3 (16.6%) the worst offenders. Only Wards 5, 11, and 12 comply.

Figure 3 — Dominant species by ward. Norway Maple dominates 12 of 15 wards. Wards 5, 11, and 13 have different dominant species, reflecting local composition variation.

Figure 4 — Maximum genus share per ward. The Acer genus exceeds the 20% cap in 14 of 15 wards. Only Ward 11 (~19.6%) stays within the guideline. Ward 4 is the worst at 31.4%.

Figure 5 — City-wide Santamour threshold overview. Points to the right of the dashed lines represent violations. Acer genus violations are near-universal; species violations affect most wards; family violations are approaching the 30% cap (Sapindaceae at 28.1%).

Figure 6 — Ward-by-ward violation summary across species, genus, and family levels. Ward 4 is the only ward with violations at all three levels simultaneously.

🛣️

Road Stress: The Urban Tree Pressure Multiplier

Street trees along arterial roads face uniquely harsh conditions: compacted soils, salt spray, exhaust pollution, frequent pruning for utility clearance, and extreme pavement heat. The road stress score weights each ward's road classification mix — arterial roads score 1.0, collector roads 0.6, local streets 0.2 — then normalizes across all wards.

Ward 3 has the highest road stress score (1.0 normalized) due to its disproportionate share of arterial roads. Ward 14 has the lowest (mostly local residential streets). Road stress is a 40% co-driver of built environment stress in the replanting formula.

Figure 7 — Normalized road stress score by ward. Ward 3 and Ward 2 lead, driven by high arterial road proportions. Ward 14 and Ward 5 have the most local-street-dominated networks.

Figure 8 — Road stress vs. zoning stress scatter plot by ward. Wards in the upper-right quadrant (high on both axes) face the most intense combined built environment pressure. Ward 3 sits in the most extreme position.

🔴

Priority Results: Ward 3 Leads Across All Horizons

Ward 3 scores 0.966 — the highest replanting priority in Hamilton, maintained identically across 2030, 2050, and 2080. Its position is driven by the maximum possible built environment stress score (1.0): 81.7% built land coverage, a normalized ISA heat index of 1.0, and the city's highest arterial road share. Combined with low species diversity, a 16.6% Norway Maple share, and moderate average tree resilience (0.498), it is unambiguously Hamilton's most urgent urban forest intervention site.

Wards 2 and 4 rank second and third. Ward 2 has the lowest species diversity of any ward (184 species) and high built stress. Ward 4 carries the steepest Acer genus dominance at 31.4% — the highest in the city.

Figure 9 — Replanting priority vs. average tree resilience quadrant (2050). Upper-left wards combine the highest replanting need with the lowest existing resilience — the strongest candidates for immediate intervention. Wards 3, 2, and 4 cluster in this critical zone.

Figure 10 — Replanting priority vs. ISA vulnerability quadrant. Wards above and to the right of the crosshair lines face both high climate stress and high replanting urgency simultaneously.

RankWardPriority ScoreTotal TreesAvg ResilienceKey Risk Driver
1Ward 30.96612,4370.498Max built stress + Max ISA heat index
2Ward 20.7799,9170.536Lowest species diversity (184 species)
3Ward 40.76021,5310.492Highest Acer genus share (31.4%)
4Ward 70.74115,7250.487Highest Norway Maple share (17.0%)
5Ward 80.72413,5600.491High built share + road stress
—Wards 9,11,12,13,15≤ 0.50variesvariesLowest vulnerability, lowest built share

Figure 11 — Urban forest risk summary dashboard: four panels showing species diversity pressure, vulnerability progression, built environment stress, and the integrated replanting priority triangle for all 15 wards.

⚠️

The Emerald Ash Borer Crisis

Hamilton's inventory contains 11,221 Fraxinus (ash) trees — 4.1% of the total canopy. The Emerald Ash Borer (Agrilus planipennis), an invasive beetle already confirmed in Ontario, kills virtually all untreated ash trees within 5–10 years of infestation. Unlike climate vulnerability which unfolds gradually, EAB represents a near-certain, near-term canopy loss event.

11,221 ash trees across all 15 wards are at imminent risk of total loss. Ward 15 is most exposed (8.9% of ward trees are ash), followed by Ward 4 (7.5%) and Ward 13 (7.1%). All Fraxinus species were excluded from the recommended replanting list — replacing ash with ash would recreate the same vulnerability.

Figure 12 — Ash tree (Fraxinus) share by ward. Ward 15 leads at 8.9%, meaning roughly 1 in 11 trees there is at imminent EAB risk. Proactive replacement planting must begin in these wards before canopy gaps form.

WardTotal TreesAsh TreesAsh ShareUrgency
Ward 1520,3171,8068.9%🔴 Highest
Ward 421,5311,6137.5%🔴 Very High
Ward 1331,4732,2327.1%🟠 High
Ward 527,3401,5135.5%🟠 High
Ward 1017,1938284.8%🟡 Moderate
🌱

Recommended Replanting Species

The top species for priority wards were selected using four strict criteria: resilience score above 0.75 (Very High or High class), not currently dominant in Hamilton's canopy (to directly reduce monoculture dependence), no Emerald Ash Borer susceptibility, and confirmed climate suitability for the Hamilton region. 9 of the top 10 recommended species introduce entirely new genera to Hamilton's urban forest.

Figure 13 — Top climate-resilient species recommended for replanting. Common Hackberry leads at 0.938 resilience score. The chart excludes all Fraxinus species. Each bar colour represents a different genus, visually confirming that the recommended list diversifies well beyond Acer.

Figure 14 — Resilience class breakdown of recommended species. All top recommendations fall in the Very High or High resilience class — ensuring that new plantings will be meaningfully more climate-adapted than the trees they replace.

SpeciesScientific NameResilienceKey Climate StrengthNew Genus?
Common HackberryCeltis occidentalis0.938 ⭐Drought, heat, pollution, flood tolerant; native✅ Yes — Celtis
Asian PearPyrus pyrifolia0.917Excellent future climate suitability rating✅ Yes — Pyrus
TanoakNotholithocarpus densiflorus0.833Very high AHS heat zone; strong drought tolerance✅ Yes — Notholithocarpus
London PlanetreePlatanus × acerifolia0.806Proven urban performer; high pollution tolerance✅ Yes — Platanus
Eastern Red CedarJuniperus virginiana0.792Native evergreen; low water; resilient to extremes✅ Yes — Juniperus

Figure 15 — Replanting priority score versus average tree resilience for all 15 wards. The quadrant analysis highlights that wards with both high replanting need and low current resilience — particularly Ward 3 — require not just more trees, but specifically the highest-resilience species available.

🗺️

Ward-Level Heat Index Snapshot Across All Horizons

This final panel brings together the ISA heat index, vulnerability by horizon, and the spatial distribution of risk — providing planners with a single consolidated view of where Hamilton needs to act, and how urgently that need escalates over time.

Figure 16 — Three-panel summary: ISA heat index by ward (left), vulnerability 2050 (centre), and replanting priority 2050 (right). The spatial concentration of risk in Ward 3 is consistent across all three metrics — it is simultaneously the hottest, most vulnerable, and highest-priority ward in Hamilton.

Climate-Driven Vulnerability Assessment of Hamilton's Urban Forest
University of Niagara Falls Canada  ·  CityLAB Hamilton
Authors: Susana Carolina Romero Aparicio · Su Myat Aung · Tessy Ramirez · Yi Xin

Replanting Priority = 0.40 × Built Environment Stress + 0.35 × Climate Vulnerability + 0.15 × Low Diversity Pressure + 0.10 × Biodiversity Pressure · Species data: Metro Vancouver Urban Forest Climate Adaptation DB
